items.q: How do you say 'I am using my phone right now' (emphasising the action is in progress)?
items.correct: Je suis en train d'utiliser mon portable.
items.why: Students translate English '-ing' as a participle. French has no progressive tense - the present 'j'utilise' covers both 'I use' and 'I am using'. For emphasis on the ongoing action, use 'être en train de + infinitive'.

items.q: How do you say 'I am going to visit Paris' (near future) in French?
items.why: Students add 'à' between aller and the infinitive. Futur proche structure is ALLER (conjugated) + INFINITIVE, with no preposition: 'je vais manger', 'je vais aller', 'je vais partir'.
A classic faux ami. Actuellement looks like 'actually' but means CURRENTLY. To say 'actually' use en fait or en réalité. These false friends regularly cost marks.

items.q: What year separated Church and State in France?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1905. “1789” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Ostentatious religious symbols in state schools
items.why: Correct answer: Ostentatious religious symbols in state schools. “All religious gatherings” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was the full-face veil banned in public spaces?
items.why: Exact answer: 2010. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
